core_concept: Ancient Rome: Republic, Empire, and Fall
facts: The Roman Republic was governed by elected officials like consuls and a Senate, but it transformed into an empire when Julius Caesar's rise and assassination led to Augustus becoming the first emperor, concentrating power in one ruler.
Augustus, originally named Octavian, ended a century of civil wars and established the Pax Romana, a long period of peace and stability that allowed trade and culture to flourish across the empire.

Christianity spread throughout the Roman Empire despite initial persecution because its message of salvation and equality appealed to many, and Emperor Constantine later legalized it, making it a major religion.

The fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 CE was not a single event but a long decline caused by economic troubles, military pressures from barbarian invasions, and political instability, leading to the last emperor being deposed.
The Roman Senate, once the center of power in the Republic, lost its authority under the empire as emperors made key decisions, though it remained an advisory body.
The Roman Empire at its height controlled the Mediterranean region, but its size made it difficult to govern, contributing to its eventual division into Western and Eastern halves.
misconceptions: Students may think Julius Caesar was the first emperor of Rome, but he was actually a dictator who never took the title of emperor; his adopted son Augustus became the first official emperor.
Students may believe the fall of the Roman Empire was a sudden collapse caused by a single invasion, when it was a gradual process over centuries with multiple contributing factors.
Students might think Christianity was always tolerated in Rome, but in fact it was illegal and Christians were persecuted for centuries before Constantine's Edict of Milan legalized it.
